1→---
     2→title: "Introduction - Designs & Engineering"
     3→part: 6
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "1934-1944"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**DESIGNS AND ENGINEERING**
     9→
    10→**Role of Designs & Engineering in a corporate**  
    11→[Design engineering plays a crucial role in corporate success by ensuring that products and systems meet both functional and aesthetic requirements. Designengineers are responsible for conceptualizing, creating, and refining products,   systems, or components, ensuring that they are not only innovative but also      feasible and efficient. They work throughout the product development life-                                                          cycle performing tasks such as designing, testing, analyzing, collaborating, and managing design changes within projects.](https://www.bing.com/ck/a?!&&p=7af17e1201bcdf993561d98ae6a8249dd652b8fb1f328f5ff1b66b6ede10d1a2JmltdHM9MTc2OTkwNDAwMA&ptn=3&ver=2&hsh=4&fclid=0d9d6691-1462-6378-00fd-70651595621a&u=a1aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuY2FyZWVyczM2MC5jb20vY2FyZWVycy9hcnRpY2xlcy9kZXNpZ24tZW5naW5lZXItam9iLWRlc2NyaXB0aW9uLXJvbGVzLWFuZC1yZXNwb25zaWJpbGl0aWVz&ntb=1)  
    12→
    13→[Design engineering is essential in various industries, including consumer            electronics, automotive, aerospace and defense, healthcare, and sustainable     energy. Design engineers are at the heart of developing cutting-edge gadgets,          energy-efficient powertrains, advanced driver-assistance systems, life-saving equipment, and renewable energy technologies. Their work significantly impacts organizational success by developing efficient manufacturing processesand consumer-focused products. ](https://www.bing.com/ck/a?!&&p=2cc996b00b0c97433c8d3909074ca72031c72fb65e90883f12c4398f84691440JmltdHM9MTc2OTkwNDAwMA&ptn=3&ver=2&hsh=4&fclid=0d9d6691-1462-6378-00fd-70651595621a&u=a1aHR0cHM6Ly9ncmlkb25zeXN0ZW1zLmNvbS93aGF0LWRvZXMtYS1kZXNpZ24tZW5naW5lZXItZG8v&ntb=1)
    14→
    15→Designs and Engineering plays a vital role on the quality, sales and cost of a product. Designs solve complex problems and create solutions. The process of designs and engineering leads to functional and optimized systems or products. It involves innovation and analytical intelligence. Designs primarily focus on functionality, performance, reliability, safety and cost. 
    16→
    17→Over designed structures are products become too costly whereas under designed structures or products are prone to failures. It is therefore very important to achieve optimized designs which will ensure quality, reliability and safety but also be competitive.
    18→

1→---
     2→title: "HIGH DESIGN COSTS"
     3→part: 6
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "1945-1957"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : HIGH DESIGN COSTS**
     9→
    10→In tenders and projects, designs must be optimized for safe and quality products yet competitive; otherwise you may lose on bids or recue profits in projects. But sometimes, designers in the Design Department live in a world of their own. They forget that their existence depends on the success of the bid and the financials of a project depends on optimized designs. They are interested in designing, say for example, a structure very safe so that it can in no way fail during testing to be conducted after award. But they fail to understand that the very award is at stake\! The very fact that the designs were not optimised and the engineers played safe while designing the same attributes to higher cost of bid which in turn may be responsible for loss of a bid\!
    11→
    12→So Design Head must ensure that he has confident and experienced engineers who can optimise designs effectively and at the same time be confident that it will pass safety and failure checks during tests conducted on award. Such confidence comes with experience but is also steered by his conscience that his very existence in the company also depends on optimized designs which would lead to competitive bids. If a bid is successful, then only a company is awarded a contract and thereafter the engineers will have work to do during the contract. If there is no job, what does the engineer do in the company, play computer games?
    13→
    14→In bids lost, it has often been found during that the designs of the contractor who won the award were much more optimized and had passed all the required tests. Designers need introspection of such designs and understand how they can improve on their designs in future. Therefore the role of designs and engineering cannot be over emphasized in winning bids or improving financials significantly of a project.
    15→
    16→**The salient points are:**
    17→
    18→1. Design Head must be cautious that his design team is making the best efforts to design products and services which will be competitive enough to win bids. On award, the design engineers may try to further optimise the designs to reap more benefits in the project financials.  
    19→2. Design Head and all his team members must develop a conscience to develop optimized designs to enable win bids. They must understand that their job is at stake if their over-designs fail to win bids.
    20→

1→---
     2→title: "NO EXPOSURE TO INTERNATIONAL DESIGN PRACTICES"
     3→part: 6
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "1958-1970"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : NO EXPOSURE TO INTERNATIONAL DESIGN PRACTICES**
     9→
    10→In international tenders, experience of foreign design practices through training programs conducted abroad and market intelligence in such countries is very much required. I remember that in a foreign tender, a designer truly followed the codes of standard for designs of that particular country and the result was that the bid was the highest priced and laughable. Much later on it was found that there were certain practices in considering some factors and assuming certain design data pertaining to that country which led to optimized design which the Design engineers never knew of. Country wise design data, design codes of practice and particularly practices prevailing on design considerations need to be known very well if a company wants to participate in international tenders.
    11→
    12→**Statistical data need to be consulted in designs.** In many cases tenders are announced where designs are more or less similar to earlier ones. Whether the earlier tenders were won or not, Design Heads, through intelligence networks, must have in hand previous design data of the competitor who is now executing the project and compare if his designs were up to the mark. I am not asking to gather this data through unethical means, but many such data can easily be obtained through observation of structures being constructed in various project sites. For example in a road construction project on one lane, someone can always observe the various activities of the construction, materials used, engineering and construction methods employed, etc from the other side of the road. Even if someone does not get all data, he can get some sense of what kind of designs went into it. After all, if you have no data, you are just groping in the dark and hoping on your luck to win\!  
    13→	  
    14→**The salient points are:**
    15→
    16→1. International designs are a different ball game. You need to be thorough with their standards and codes of practice.  
    17→2. Also importantly, design practices and data that are assumed in standard designs need to be known.  
    18→3. For the above, training abroad for designs and practices adopted in the country may be recommended.   
    19→4. Statistical data of designs and engineering must be collected for reference for finalising designs similar in nature to earlier designs.
    20→

1→---
     2→title: "SOME GOOD PRACTICES"
     3→part: 6
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "1971-1980"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : SOME GOOD PRACTICES**
     9→
    10→**Compile competitor based technical data**  
    11→You may lose many bids, after all 10% win of bids submitted is considered to be a decent performance in Bidding. And, other competitors are no fools\! So, during or after the completion of the projects by its competitors, the Design Manager with the help of Marketing Manager must collect insights and technical data of the project implemented by others. While collecting data of other competitors may be considered to be unethical, it is debated by many that when a project is already implemented and not restricted to public’s eye, there is nothing wrong in collecting data through visual inspections. 
    12→
    13→Above data will provide insights as to whether the bids lost was because of un-optimised designs based on which the bid cost got escalated. In such cases, the Bidding Manager has to introspect in similar designs later on in future bids.
    14→
    15→**Train in international software and practices**  
    16→Sometimes renowned software may be costly but so appropriate that you may win some bids because of that. Such wins will compensate for the costs incurred to procure such software. If a bidder participates in international tenders, the Design engineers must also train themselves in international practices to design using international design codes
    17→

1→---
     2→title: "Introduction - Bidding & Costing"
     3→part: 7
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "1983-2000"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**BIDDING AND COSTING**
     9→
    10→**The role of Bidding and Costing in a corporate**  
    11→Bidding serves as a strategic tool to enhance business operations and drive growth. It allows businesses to identify potential projects that align with their capabilities and goals and expand market opportunities. It showcases an organization’s strengths and expertise, quality of work and therefore retains clients due such value proposition. Bidding facilitates the best terms and condition to select the most appropriate product or service. 
    12→
    13→Bidding is crucial in an organization to win and secure new businesses. It involves meticulous planning, documenting, reviwing and submitting competitive and responsive proposals to ensure compliance and competitive bids. 
    14→
    15→Costing plays a crucial role by providing detail financial insights that guide competitive costs for winning contracts. Such costs must be reliable and executable keeping the profit margins intact as envisaged and planned.    
    16→Costing is one of the most important departments in an organisation because this department determines the future of the organization by securing orders. Only after securing contracts, all other departments can execute and perform their own functions in line with the requirements of the contract.
    17→
    18→Bidding & Costing is facilitated by three more important Departments which are the Strategy, Marketing & Business Development and Advertising. All these departments strive continuously to facilitate order book but if the Bidding & Costing department fails to acquire order through its bids, then there is no work for any other department.
    19→
    20→In short, Strategy, Marketing & Business Development and Advertising facilitate Bidding & Costing Department to prepare and submit bids or auctions to book order.
    21→
    22→The other departments execute their major functions once orders are booked. For example, the Accounts department keeps a track of the costs being incurred in the organisation, HR may strategise to recruit people in office and at project sites; SCM may create new vendor partnerships; Quality & Safety departments will ensure quality and safety of the project under execution; so on and so forth. However the major role of all these departments comes into play once orders are booked.
    23→

1→---
     2→title: "HAVING LION’S EYE, NOT AN EAGLE’S"
     3→part: 7
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "2001-2039"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : HAVING LION’S EYE, NOT AN EAGLE’S**
     9→
    10→Do you know how many hours does a lion sleep? It’s around 18 to 20 hours. The balance time in a day is for hunting an eating. So a lion is basically a very lazy animal.
    11→
    12→Also do you know the power of the eyesight of an eagle? Its expertise in picking up its moving target on ground is highly impressive, just like a kingfisher picks up a fish from a river within seconds.
    13→
    14→Why am I talking about all these animal instincts? I will tell you why in just a while.
    15→
    16→A tender comprises of techno commercial and financial specifications, may run into thousands of pages which a bidder has to carefully go through and prepare his bid. Ignoring any important line or clause in the specifications may cost the organisation dearly. There are a multiple examples of this. I will cite some.
    17→
    18→The first example is that of mine. When I was the BD Manager, a tender for construction of a particular Transmission Line was announced. I prepared a good techno commercial and price bid and submitted the same to the client. Later on I was shocked to know that our bid was rejected. One of the lines amongst thousands of pages of specifications read that the bidder must be a manufacturer of tower structures, which we were not at that point of time. I somehow missed the line while I read the voluminous bidding documents. And this cost the organisation dearly because had our bid opened we would have been L1 and secured the order.  
    19→   
    20→In another example, there were two tenders related to the same project, the first tender for the first geographical portion of the project and the second one for the next geographical portion of the same project, say Tender 1 and Tender 2\. To maintain secrecy of the final bid price,   
    21→the Head asked the junior engineers to prepare bid prices with escalated rates for all items, the intention being that he will submit a discount vide a discount letter and attach with the bid to arrive at the final bid price. The Head wanted to attach a 5% discount letter for Tender 1 and a 15% discount letter for Tender 2 to arrive at his intended final bid prices. The Head then did a massive blunder. He put the 15% discount letter in Tender 1 and the 5% discount letter in Tender 2\. So what happened is that the Tender 1 submitted became undervalued (ie. would incur loss when executed) and Tender 2 became overvalued. The result was that the organisation won the contract against Tender 1 and lost lot of money after it was executed. And Tender 2 being overvalued was lost. Remember, after a bid is submitted or awarded, you cannot rectify your mistakes. That is the rule of bids and awards.
    22→
    23→Another example was very interesting. The specifications said that the bids must be submitted on so and so date by 9.30 am. The bid had to be submitted on the third floor of the client’s office building. Many bidders submitted their bid by 9.30 am; however it was astonishing to find that a very competitive bidder had not submitted its bid by that time. Many bidders were happy at the thought that the competition would be less. However, all of a sudden from nowhere, this competitive bidder came running and submitted his bid at 9.35 am. All other bidders represented that this late bid should not be accepted by the client. However, this bidder represented that he had entered the client’s office and registered his attendance at 9.28 am but then he waited for the lift in the building and it was 9.35 am by the time he could submit the bid. Amidst this confusion, the client immediately instituted a committee of directors and finally the committee directed that the bid be accepted since the bidder had registered his attendance in the office of reception of the client at 9.28 am, thereby his bid will not be considered as late. 
    24→
    25→The English of the specifications can sometimes be confusing leading to rejection of bids. For example, one specification stated “the contractor must have experience in three similar transmission line projects of minimum 100 kms which involved supply of conductors and insulators in the project”. Now there can be different interpretations like this:
    26→
    27→1. Do all the three projects required supply of conductors and insulators both?  
    28→2. Does it mean that one project could have supplied insulators only and the other two supplied conductors?  
    29→3. What happens if one of transmission line had no supply of conductors or insulators but the other two had?  
    30→4. Is it that each of the transmission line should have been 100 kms long?  
    31→5. Is it that the cumulative length of all the transmission lines should be atleast 100 kms long?
    32→
    33→Sometimes the English used in specifications can be distorted and differently interpreted by different bidders. In such cases, it is best to raise clarifications to the client in writing and act accordingly. 
    34→
    35→Numerous such interesting examples can be given. But in essence what I am trying to drive at is that the Bidding & Costing Department must keep their eyes open and has to be as sharp as that of an eagle’s. You cannot be lazy like a lion and miss the details of a bid. In all cases of blunders cited above, they were cases of oversight. Heads and employees of Bidding & Costing cannot be excused for such oversights because you lose lot of opportunities for the organisation and at times lose lot of money for such oversights.
    36→
    37→Because of my oversight in the first case, I was reprimanded. However, it was a big learning for me to be extremely careful thereafter and always read specifications with an eagle’s eye. I never used to read specifications if I was stressed or not feeling like to read at any point of time.
    38→
    39→HR must ensure that Heads and managers in Bidding & Costing are very meticulous in reading specifications without oversight. You must be an eagle, not a lion.
    40→
    41→**The salient points of concern are:**
    42→
    43→1. A bidding engineer must be meticulous in studying the bid documents  
    44→2. Submission of documents for different tenders must be carefully packaged so as to not mix documents amongst tenders  
    45→3. If a clause arise multiple interpretations, ask clarifications in writing to the client
    46→

1→---
     2→title: "BEING THE RODENT, NOT THE OWL"
     3→part: 7
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "2040-2061"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : BEING THE RODENT, NOT THE OWL**
     9→
    10→The rodent is known as one of the most impatient animal in the world. Every second it wants to move in search of food. On the contrary the owl is the most rare patient spirit animal and creature. In case you encounter an owl, the universe is giving you a sign. This animal represents wisdom, patience, solitude, deep connection and intuitive knowledge.  
    11→   
    12→In the department of Bidding & Costing, one needs prolonged concentration and patience to go through the huge bidding documents minutely keeping a note of all the important clauses and terms of the specifications based on which the techno-commercial bid and the price bid would be prepared.
    13→
    14→There are men who are often distracted during work for different reasons including time off for smoking. There are women who are also often distracted for various reasons including time off for facial touch ups. I am not discouraging either of them, but what I am trying to mean is that people in this department needs to have deep concentration for prolonged periods before breaks so that they do not miss the minute details of the documents. It has been observed that employees resorting to regular breaks often miss some important points of the documents, particularly at the point they left reading before the break and commence reading after the break.   
    15→   
    16→Once, someone took a break while being on the middle of a chapter while going through a technical document. After the break, he resumed browsing the specifications, but missed a paragraph while doing so. The paragraph contained an important product parameter to be considered in the bid, which was ignored during the preparation of the bid. It’s a simple guess for what happened after the submission of the bid. The bid was technically rejected.
    17→
    18→In another instance, I was anxious about someone’s health while I was reading a specification requirement that the bidder has to be a manufacturer for a particular product. I did not understand its importance probably due to lack of concentration and I did not make this a point to be considered in the bid. Eventually the bid was rejected.  
    19→
    20→So specifications have to be read minutely in order not to miss any points in any chapter. It helps to make your technical and commercial bid responsive. HR and top leaders must ensure that other than the requisites of an employee to work in the Bidding department, prolonged patience to go through huge bidding documents is a must. 
    21→
    22→You must display the characteristics of an owl being patient and concentrating on every move, not the rodent being distracted here and there every moment.  
    23→
    24→**The salient points of concern are:**
    25→
    26→1. A bidding engineer must have ample patience to read voluminous bidding documents.  
    27→2. A bidding engineer must love reading bidding documents. If you do not like reading after a while, you are bound to miss some important points.  
    28→3. Many engineers do not have the power of concentration. They will not be a good fit in the bidding department. This is not to say that they are bad, they may be fit well in some other departments.
    29→

1→---
     2→title: "DIVULGENCE FOR GAINS"
     3→part: 7
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "2062-2076"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : DIVULGENCE FOR GAINS**
     9→
    10→Bidding and Costing is a sensitive department. Every detail needs to be kept secret because you are competing with the bids of others. So any information leaked from your bid to your competitor may prove to be very dangerous leading to loss of bids.
    11→
    12→There are some scrupulous people who take advantage of this. They pass on secret information of bid and costs to other competitors in lieu of unethical money. These people are dishonest and are not loyal to their own company. They know that they can earn good money by such unethical means though their company may not be able to win tenders. If situation comes that the company is struggling for existence due to continuous loss of bids, they would switch over to some other companies and resort to malpractices there also.
    13→
    14→Bidding team members must keep a thorough and a minute watch if such malpractice is adopted by any team member. HR and the Vigilance team must also keep a close watch. Bid opening results for certain bids may also provide a clue if something of this sort is happening in the department. The Marketing Head should monitor which bid has been prepared by which engineer leading to bid losses despite preparing competitive bids. If required, employees may be interrogated for foul play.
    15→
    16→It may so happen that other department employees roam freely in the bidding department and get hold of certain crucial data which can be passed on to a competitor for vested interest. Therefore this department must have a separate glass enclosure within the office premises with permission to access by other department employees if need be.
    17→
    18→**The salient points of concern are:**
    19→
    20→1. The Marketing Head, HR, Vigilance team and bidding team members must be vigilant if any data is being leaked outside by any employee for personal gains.  
    21→2. The bidding area must have a separate enclosure with locked doors making it inaccessible to any other department. Permission to access the bidding department may be on need basis.
    22→

1→---
     2→title: "MALPRACTICE OF MISCOSTING"
     3→part: 7
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "2077-2099"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : MALPRACTICE OF MISCOSTING**
     9→
    10→Winning a bid against competitors is a tough job. A decent strike rate for winning tenders could be between 10% and 15% where there are a number of competitors in the fray.
    11→
    12→Sometimes a incapable bidding engineer, though might have been recruited based on interviews earlier, fails to prepare competitive bids. So they resort to malpractice as follows.
    13→
    14→In a complicated bid like that for an EPC project, there are thousands of cost data to be analysed and derived. For example, for a simple item like concreting a floor slab of a building, you need to know the costs of cement, sand, coarse aggregates, reinforcement of different sizes, wires to bind the reinforcement, transportation costs of all these materials from their sources to the nearest point for construction, head-loading cost of these materials from the nearest road point to the actual site, cost of shuttering, placement of reinforcement, mixing concrete, usage of equipment like mixer, vibrator, labour costs, etc.
    15→
    16→Similarly, there are hundreds of items in a complicated project where thousands of data has to be analysed. It is not possible for a Head to leave all his work and check all these data analysed by the bidding engineer. Taking advantage of this, a unethical engineer, in order to try to win a bid, may intentionally consider lesser cost of materials than it is so in reality, and make the bid competitive, however rendering it un-executable financially. The bidding engineer may be further cunning enough to manipulate these costs of items which would actually be executed at the end of the project execution. For example, the electrical and sanitary fittings are executed at the end of the project execution.
    17→
    18→The advantage is that the bidding engineer gets sufficient time to plan his course of action before getting caught. Bid evaluation of a project may take 4 to 6 months, another month for award, designs and engineering may take 4 to 6 months of time, and thereafter the item of construction where the bidding engineer may have manipulated would come up after another year. So it’s almost two years that the manipulated item of construction is taken up. Therefore the engineer gets two years time before the company understands that the items have been under quoted. Two years is a long time by which the engineer would have mal-practiced by under quoting in many bids. Many such bids would have been awarded and during this time of two years the engineer may have been congratulated, facilitated, awarded and promoted with good bonuses\! Then when the time comes after two years before he is exposed, he switches over to other companies in case he is accused for such under quoting or carries on in the company if no punishment is given to him.
    19→
    20→The Head, by virtue of his position, must be aware of such malpractices. He has estimates of different costs because of his experience. So he should look into the final costs, instead of going into the details for which time would not be available) that engineer has prepared before finalising the bid. If the Head gives time to overview the costing before a bid is submitted, he can easily detect if under quoting has been resorted to.
    21→
    22→Also after a bid is won, the Project manager assigned for the construction of the project after award may go through all the item costs right from the beginning to end to understand if the costing of all the items have been done rightly.
    23→
    24→**The salient points of concern are:**
    25→
    26→1. Some bidding engineer may resort to under quote some items of the bid intentionally to be lower price and win bids.  
    27→2. Such bids when awarded are actually un-executable financially.  
    28→3. Marketing Head must browse all item costs with his experience before the bid is submitted to understand if any malpractice has been adopted.  
    29→4. The assigned Project Manager, on bid becoming L1, can validate cost of all items. However, any malpractice of under quote found at this stage will put the bidder in financial crisis since the bidder cannot change prices at this point of time.
    30→

1→---
     2→title: "THE EXTRA TIME AND VALIDTY PROBLEM"
     3→part: 7
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "2100-2120"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : THE EXTRA TIME AND VALIDTY PROBLEM**
     9→
    10→Sometimes upon request of the bidder in order to complete a bid preparation or because of the requirement of a client, the bid submission time is extended. The bidder in the meantime may have received offers from vendors which are valid for the required period of time as per the original specification. For example, say a bid was supposed to be submitted on 1st January of any year with a bid validity of 3 months, ie. Valid till the end of March 31st; but the time of bid submission is extended to 15th January by the client with the same bid terms, then the different mistakes that the bidder often overlooks are the following.
    11→
    12→1. The vendors and subcontractors may have submitted their offers with validity till 31st March. But with the extension of time of bid submission by 15 days; the offers required validity till 15th April in line with client’s terms. But the same might have been ignored by the bidder. Now if the award is given by the client say on 10th April, then the offers of the vendors at this time of award is not valid. The vendor can rightly say that their offer is not valid if it finds that the offer terms and prices are not appropriate during this period of time due to cost escalations. The vendor has every right to change its prices and terms after its original validity and therefore the bidder is in deep trouble.
    13→
    14→2. Another mistake that a bidder does is that, even if the time for bid submission is not extended by the client, the bidder does not ask for a validity of offer from their vendors and subcontractors for at least one month more beyond the validity of the bid. This practice should be resorted to because if the client places the order on 31st March (ie. Within bid validity period), thereafter the SCM department may take some time, say a month, to negotiate and finalize the order with the vendor. Now if the offer validity of the vendor was till March 31st, then the same problem crops up as cited above.  
    15→   
    16→
    17→However, there is an important point to note. Sometimes a client allows variable prices of items with respect to time based on a given formula. If such formulas really take care of the cost escalation over time, then there is nothing to worry. But if the bid was quoted with firm prices and the bid is extended based on client’s requirement, then there is a cause of worry in case the cost escalations are beyond the fixed prices quoted. In such a case, asking for more validity from vendors makes sense.  
    18→Another important point to note is that after the bid is submitted and the bid is L1, and thereafter the client has procedural delays and intends to award beyond the validity period, the client will ask the bidder to extend his bid validity. In such a case, the bidder has every right not to extend his bid if it finds that it cannot sustain the cost escalations taken place over this period of time until it is compensated by the price variation formula of the client. 
    19→
    20→The irony is if the bidder does not wish to extend the validity of its bid after the bid is submitted which it has every legal right to do so, the client is often angry and tries to convince the bidder to extend its bid. This is because the client knows you have given the best price and therefore needs your extension of validity of the bid. Also usually, the client requires three valid techno-commercial responsive bids in order to award a bidder. 
    21→
    22→**The salient points of concern are:**
    23→
    24→1. Ask for one more month of validity from the vendors and subcontractors than that required in the bid so that SCM gets one month to negotiate and place orders in case the client awards the project at the fag end of the validity period of the bid.   
    25→   2. Ensure that the vendors and contractors do not quote higher because you have asked for one month of validity of their offers.  
    26→      3. As and when the client extends the bid validity before the bids are submitted, the bidder should ask similar extensions of offer validities from the vendors and the contractors.  
    27→         4. In case of bid validity extensions before bid is submitted, bidders must evaluate cost escalations vis-a-vis the price variation formula the client has stipulated in the bid. In case of fixed price bids, bidders need to carefully evaluate based on extensions of validity given by their vendors.
    28→

1→---
     2→title: "THE COMPLETION TIME ISSUE"
     3→part: 7
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "2121-2142"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : THE COMPLETION TIME ISSUE**
     9→
    10→In tender documents, the completion period required for executing a project is stipulated. The contractor may be executing many such similar projects at different sites. The Bid Manager may notice that the time taken to execute such projects or similar projects under execution is far more than the time stipulated in the current bid pertaining to a similar project.
    11→
    12→The Bid Manager consults with the Project Manager and understands that the Project Management costs in the bid must be considered for some more months than stipulated in the bid due to anticipated longer duration for completion. However, this will result in higher cost and therefore the bid may become uncompetitive. Even with the stipulated period of completion, the Bid Manager could consider “Crashing” of the project, in which case again the bid becomes uncompetitive.
    13→
    14→Sometimes in such cases there arises a conflict between the Bid Manager and the Project Manager. The Bid Manager, to become competitive, intends to consider the stipulated completion period for the Project Management Costs asking the Project team to become more efficient henceforth, whereas the Project Manager wants that the Bid Manager consider some more months for project completion because he anticipates a longer duration for project execution. And sometimes they quarrel and fight\!\!\!
    15→
    16→The above scenario happens in many organisations. The basic problem here is that the Project team thinks that winning bids is not his responsibility and his KRA, promotions and increment shall be irrespective of bids won or lost by the Bidding team. Therefore, in one of my chapters, I have raised a serious issue; is only the Bidding team responsible for winning or losing bids? Or, equally responsible are the Designs cell, the Project cell, the Finance cell who provides key data affecting the overall bid cost?
    17→
    18→The Project team also fails to note one very important aspect of business. If a Bidding team does not win bids, there will be no projects to be executed by the Project team. An organization generates turnover and profits and thereby provides salaries only when projects are executed. Without project execution, none gets salary including the Project team. And without bidding competitively, the organization does not win projects. It is therefore in the interest of all employees of all departments and the organization that everyone support the Bidding team to be competitive. To do so, all departments must find means to be more efficient. Project Team may also need to find means to be more effective and complete projects in stipulated period of time so that the bidding team does not unnecessarily consider higher Project management costs and become uncompetitive.
    19→
    20→In today’s world, cost of inefficiency cannot be considered in bid. If you are inefficient and escalate costs because of your efficiency, you will cease to be a player in the market. Either you be efficient or perish in the market. Let not a bid become uncompetitive because of inefficiency of Project team or any other department who contribute significantly to bid costs.
    21→
    22→**The salient points of concern are:**
    23→
    24→1. Since bidding team have to put their most competitive bid, Project team should not insist Bidding team to consider more months of project execution than stipulated in the bid until and unless it is really practically not feasible.  
    25→   2. Project team and all other departments must find efficient means to provide their most competitive costs to the bidding team for them to consider in their bids.  
    26→      3. In today’s competitive environment, cost of inefficiency cannot be considered in a bid. If you do so, you will cease to be in the industry.
    27→

1→---
     2→title: "THE ONLY CULPRIT FOR LOSING BIDS"
     3→part: 7
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "2143-2161"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : THE ONLY CULPRIT FOR LOSING BIDS**
     9→
    10→The Marketing or the Bidding Department of an organisation is reprimanded for failure to secure orders. Yes, they are the final decider of the price bids submitted by them, hence reprimanded for losses. But the story should not end here.
    11→
    12→There are several costs built up in a bid. Manpower costs are primarily driven by HR. The more optimised, effective and experienced manpower are recruited by HR, the more the bid cost will be optimised. Supply Chain (SC) Costs is another major cost built up in a bid. The SCM Department is responsible for evaluating and recommending the best responsive yet cost competitive vendors in the bid. Failing to do so will jack up bid prices. Finance is another significant contributor to bid cost. Finance Department must have the best negotiated fianance costs with partner banks so that the cash flow finance costs built up in a bid are as less as possible. The overhead costs of the organisation must be reasonable. Often it is found that the top heavy management increases the overheads and therefore the bid cost. Logistics cost provided by the Fleet Department should also be optimised. The right technology and equipment to be used during the project execution as determined by the Technology Department also has a reasonable share in the bid cost. And very importantly, it is the Designs Department who provide design data based on which product and service costs are determined and considered in a bid. Bad designs alone can render a bid highly uncompetitive in spite of other factors being optimised. The Marketing team too provides the proportionate cost of marketing and advertising in each bid.
    13→
    14→Therefore, I find it amusing that during appraisals, the Bidding Department is either facilitated or punished alone for securing or not being able to secure orders. The Bidding team actually compiles costs obtained from various departments and forms a bid just like a music director composes the singing with other instruments to form a song. However, since the bidding team is responsible to have a market intelligence of probable winning levels, its role to negotiate and accept costs from all other departments cannot be under estimated. At the same time, other departments who provide and contribute to bid costs cannot be ignored and be scot free for bid losses. 
    15→
    16→Each department must be accountable for a proportionate percentage to its contribution to the bid cost. And similar weightages may be given to all departments for bid wins and losses. This will drive all departments to be most cost effective since the bid results will affect their appraisals too. Otherwise what happens is these Departments play safe and provide slightly higher values to the bidding department so that if the organisation wind the bid and secures an order, then they can execute their portion of work easily without hard work or struggle. All departments need to provide optimised costs so that they struggle and work hard to achieve such targets. In other words all departments need to be cost efficient. 
    17→
    18→Appraising all departments in a manner as illustrated above will make all cost efficient. 
    19→
    20→**The salient points of concern are:**
    21→
    22→1. All departments have a critical role to provide costs to the bidding team to develop the final bid costs. Therefore only the bidding team cannot be reprimanded for bid loss or appreciated for bid wins. All departments need to take accountability for bid wins and losses.  
    23→2. Each department has a proportionate percentage of contribution to bid costs. Therefore in annual appraisals, these weightages may be considered for bid wins and losses to bring in accountability of all departments for bids.  
    24→3. Once there is accountability of each department of bid costs, all departments would tend to find means to be competitive.  
    25→4. In case of bid wins, it is not that all departments were competitive. Conversely in case of bid losses, it is not that all departments were uncompetitive. Leaders must find out from time to time from their experience the competitive levels of each department that they should achieve.
    26→

1→---
     2→title: "Some Good Practices"
     3→part: 7
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "2162-2212"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**SOME GOOD PRACTICES**
     9→
    10→**Cost is reality. But price is a strategy**  
    11→Price is not necessarily always based on cost. It is also based on the perceived value and the USP of the product or service of the client. If there is such a strong USP that competitors do not have, you have the liberty to jack up your prices to reap more profits. Therefore check on your client’s perception before you finalise your price. You may price your product or service differently for different clients based on their varied perceptions.  
    12→
    13→**Prepare your customer segments strategically**  
    14→Different customers require different value propositions. Your price must incorporate such value propositions in your product or service. Therefore the basically same product or service needs to be tweaked for different value propositions.
    15→
    16→**Follow the 80-20 rule**  
    17→Often you will find that 80% of your profits are obtained from 20% of your customers. So spend more time to these 20% customers and do not consume more time for the 80% customers. 
    18→
    19→**Check for vendor offer validity before submission**  
    20→You must check validity of offers of the vendors not only up to the latest bid validity period but further to accommodate time for SCM to place orders to the vendors 
    21→
    22→**Check for PQ criteria and documentation once again before submission**  
    23→This is important because a client may not give you a second chance to revise your technical bid. Incorrect documentation can lead to rejection of bids, however good your prices may be competitive. Your bid need to be technically responsive.
    24→
    25→**Correct your English**  
    26→Spelling mistakes, poor grammar and unclear language in bid proposals reflect a bad image of your organization. This may deter your bid to win projects.
    27→
    28→**Effective Supplier Management**  
    29→Poor supply management may lead to cost inefficiencies and increased cost which will subsequently have animpact on the overall bid cost.  
    30→**Cost Control Management**  
    31→Effective cost control measures must be implemented to optimise cost. A Bid do not have the luxury to consider costs of inefficiency 
    32→
    33→**Check unit prices carefully**  
    34→Check unit prices before bid submission. Wrongly stated unit prices due to decimal point here and there, for example Rs 104.23 instead of Rs 1042.30 or Rs 100.00 instead of Rs 1000.00, cannot be corrected by the bidder once the bid is submitted. A client can correct the total amount if wrongly calculated but will not allow the bidder to correct the unit prices.
    35→
    36→**Check your discount letter properly**  
    37→If there were two similar Packages submitted under the same tender call, ie. Say Package A and Package B, and you provide a discount on a particular item mentioning the serial number of Package B (not intended) instead of Package A (intended), then the discount will be applied accordingly and the bidder will be in a problem. The client will not allow the bidder to rectify the Package name in the discount letter. 
    38→
    39→**Check the bid security meticulously**  
    40→Read in between lines and words of the bid security issued by your bank and the format given by the client in the bidding documents. Any significant difference will lead to rejection of bid. Such bid securities will be returned back to the bidder in due course of time.
    41→
    42→**Reach on time to the place and venue of bid submission**  
    43→There have been cases where a bid has been rejected when the bidder arrives at the venue of bid submission even after a minute of the due submission time. Excuses like traffic, etc will not be considered by the client.
    44→
    45→**Put a third Bidding Engineer for review before bid submission**  
    46→A third engineer, who was not involved in the bid preparation, may check the overall preparation of the bid and correct errors before submission of the bid
    47→
    48→**Review fixed prices once again**  
    49→For fixed cost bids, the Bidding Manager must review the process of arriving at fixed prices once again and modify them if required before submission of the bid since he will not be able to change these fixed prices once the bid is submitted.
    50→
    51→**Review finally prepared bid by a third engineer**  
    52→In my tenure, I always had put a third engineer, ie. a engineer who was not involved in preparation of the bid, to broadly look into the requirements of the bidding documents and scrutiny the bid prepared by the concerned engineer. Invariably this third engineer used to find certain mistakes or discrepancies in the bid prepared which were subsequently corrected before the submission of the bid. I understood how important it was to review a prepared bid before submission.
    53→
    54→**Varied documentation**  
    55→Bids may be domestically funded by the client itself or a project may be multilaterally funded by Asian Development Bank (ADB), World Bank, JBIC and many other financial institutions like this.
    56→
    57→Differently funded projects may require different documentation for a bid to be submitted. One must be careful in studying bid documents minutely to understand the varied nature of documents required.
    58→

     2→title: "Introduction - Project Management"
     3→part: 8
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "2215-2222"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**PROJECT MANAGEMENT**
     9→
    10→**Role of Project Management in a corporate**  
    11→The main role is to ensure completion of projects on time within budgeted costs maintaining quality, safety and ethical standards of the organization. Successful completion of a project requires meticulous planning, monitoring and execution including a successful financial closure. Facilitating effective communication channels and decision making plays an important role in the team so that potential issues are identified and addressed promptly.   
    12→
    13→A project has multiple dimensions. First and foremost, the SBU Head has to brief about the project to all its stake holders. Next, the Bidding team has to produce the background of the bid in details which forms the basis of the awarded project. Next, the planner has to conceive and plan various stages of execution. It’s often said that a well-planned event is half the job done. The HR team has to put in place the different positions as per the project organogram. The Administration has to see that all requirements of transport, stationary, ticketing is in place. The Finance has to plan the financing of the project. The Designs and Engineering team have to design and test all relevant structures before the SCM team orders for mass production. The SCM team has to procure materials on time. The IT department has to provide adequate IT infrastructure at the Head office and at the project site. The Audit team should be up in their heels for checks and balances. The Accounts has to keep the daily account of project costs and revenues. The Fleet & Equipment team has to provide the required machinery and tools to the site. The Store’s manager has to prepare to store materials for construction from where they will be dispatched to the site in a regulated manner. The Commercial department has to raise invoices. The Insurance team has to arrange various insurances such as Project insurance, vehicle insurance, third party insurance, etc. The Contracts manager has to ensure that the project adheres to all the contractual liabilities as stipulated in the signed contract. The Quality team at site has to ensure adoption of quality methods during construction. The Safety team at site has to ensure safety for all its colleagues, contractors and labour. The Sustainability team has to provide access to green energy. The Ethics team has to ensure that all means and methods adopted for the construction of the project is through ethical standards.  
    14→What the above means. A project is not just mere construction as many Project Manager thinks. Yes, a project is definitely the core of the construction process but surrounding it all departments are actively involved to facilitate the construction of the project.
    15→

1→---
     2→title: "MISCONCEPTS OF PROJECT MANAGEMENT"
     3→part: 8
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "2223-2254"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : MISCONCEPTS OF PROJECT MANAGEMENT**
     9→
    10→I have observed many “so called able” Project Managers in office think that their job is to facilitate the construction and progress at site. Almost the total time they work is devoted to site. They discuss with site engineers at site, the contractors, and the labour force and monitor the various sources of raw materials like cement, sand, etc.
    11→
    12→As illustrated earlier, they also interact with HR, Administration, SCM, Finance, IT and many other departments because they need the required resources from these departments. 
    13→
    14→So all over the time they concentrate primarily on the construction and the requirements for such construction from several departments. That is why they also spend lot of time at sites to ensure progress. What they miss out most importantly is the Profitability and progress of the project. Progress of a project is very important, but NOT AT THE COST of profitability and time. They must sit with Accounts and Finance from time to time to keep a track of the profitability and the construction time spent for the project. Let me discuss these two very important aspects one by one.
    15→
    16→Project Managers must understand that the final objective of an organisation is not to execute projects, but to earn money through execution of projects. Therefore Project execution is only a medium to earn money. You cannot lose sight of the final objective of an organisation. Yes, you must track the construction progress, no one disputes that, but simultaneously you have to track at what costs you are progressing the projects. Profitability has to be tracked from time to time. Project Managers have to discuss with their seniors and Finance and track the Profitability vis-à-vis as envisaged during approved bid submission. Lot of time has to be spent in thinking how to sustain and improve profitability. The actual execution of the project is the SOLE duty of the site manager whereas reviewing the execution at site is ONE OF THE duties of a Project Manager in office. 
    17→
    18→Also remember, that a project may earn around 5% to 8% net margin, almost always on single digit figure, which means that the total cost, direct costs and indirect/overhead costs are 92% to 95% of the contract value. The retention money in Payment terms of the client is usually 10%. So during the entire course of project execution, you can raise invoices of only 90% of the contract value (10% being retention) whereas you need to spend 92% to 95% of the contract value as costs. This means you are “out of pocket” during the entire course of project execution right from the beginning to end. You earn your 5% margin only when you get back your retention money after completion of the project. Your profit is actually embedded in the portion of retention money and this is Free Cash. Free Cash supports an organization to provide salary, bonus and all the perks and perquisites that the company offers to you. 
    19→
    20→Therefore use of money must be treated with caution. If unused erroneously, your cost of financing goes up, your overall cost goes up, further reducing your planned profit.
    21→
    22→Next important factor is the time of execution. Suppose a Rs 100 Cr project earns Rs 5 Cr net margin in a scheduled one year completion period. So the company is supposed to earn Rs 5 Cr in a particular year. Now say, the project progresses at a slow pace and is completed in three years but with a better margin of Rs 6 Cr. A better margin could have come with better negotiations of vendors and services, though project management costs would be higher for three years as against one year.
    23→
    24→In the above case, the organisation would still be unhappy despite earning higher profits. Do you know why? Simple. Earning Rs 6 Cr net margin in a span of 3 years means that the company earned, on an average Rs 2 Cr per year whereas it was supposed to earn Rs 5 Cr in the scheduled one year completion. Therefore the company earns much less than envisaged per year. Remember, earning for the company in stipulated time is the final objective, not the project execution. Project execution is only a medium to earn that money. And you have to maximise these earnings as far as possible every year since a company’s financials are audited and declared every year and such results convey confidence to the equity stakeholder in case of a listed company. Even if a company is not listed, its annual financial results are of utmost importance.
    25→
    26→**The salient points of concern are:**
    27→
    28→1. What we understand from above is that profit and time are the essence of a company’s objective, not the project execution.   
    29→2. A project execution is only a medium to earn money  
    30→3. Also importantly, profit and time are interrelated. Profits of a project per year are important rather than total profits at the end of the project.  
    31→4. Progress of a project is very important, but NOT AT THE COST of profitability and time  
    32→5. The actual execution of the project is the SOLE duty of the site manager whereas reviewing the execution of a project at site is ONE OF THE duties of a Project Manager in office.   
    33→6. Your profit is actually embedded in the portion of retention money  
    34→7. During Warranty period after the completion of project, if rework due to defects has to be done, then profitability dwindles further  
    35→8. Actual profit is therefore derived only after warranty period, ie. On financial closure of the project.  
    36→9. A Project Manager has to facilitate project execution through the support of various departments while keeping a track of the site execution. The Manager cannot simply use his entire for reviewing site progress.
    37→

1→---
     2→title: "INTENTIONAL IGNORANCE OF BID COST"
     3→part: 8
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "2255-2278"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : INTENTIONAL IGNORANCE OF BID COST**
     9→
    10→This is an intentional conflict created by the Project Manager rendering suspicion on the Bid Manager. Let me explain with an example.
    11→
    12→Suppose a Bid manager had submitted a bid for Rs 140 Cr and have eventually been awarded the project by the client. The bid may have contained hundreds of item prices with unit prices and total prices mentioned against each item. Assume that the quantities of each of these items were stipulated by the client and the bidder provided the unit and the total prices. Also assume that the bidder had approved a cost (direct and indirect including overhead costs) of Rs 133 Cr from the management, in other words, there was a Rs 7 cr profit (5%) built up in the bid.
    13→
    14→After the award, what does the Project Manager do? He knows that he has to do a lot of hard work along with the SCM team to arrive at the highly competitive costs from the vendors and other subcontractors. After all, if a bid has to win it has to go for very competitive prices but achievable. But the Project manager takes a “go easy” attitude and considers higher than bid costs for all unit costs against each item to be safe so that he does not over run on costs during the execution stage. Therefore during the project initiation stage, the Project manager arrives at a total cost of Rs 136, leaving Rs 4 Cr (2.85%) as envisaged profit. The Project Manager thereafter submits this budget to the management.
    15→
    16→What happens next is that the management questions the Bid Manager as to why his estimated project cost of Rs 133 Cr becomes Rs 136 Cr now as envisaged by the Project manager, thus reducing the profitability of the project from 5% to 2.85%? The Bid manager does not agree to this and blames the Project manager for considering safe and comfortable costs as against competitive costs. The Project manager argues that the competitive costs considered by the Bid manager are not practical. So the top management is now in the midst of a conflict and asks both the managers to discuss and come up with a final budget.
    17→
    18→The above problem can be solved in the following manner. In some organisations, after award of a project, the Bid manager is not a part of the Procurement team. This is a fallacy. The Bid manager must be a part of the procurement team along with the Project Manager because firstly the Bid Manager is responsible for costs envisaged for the bid submitted and secondly the Bid manager can witness for himself whether the costs negotiated after award are the most competitive costs that the Project manager should consider.
    19→
    20→In many cases it has been observed that the suppliers and subcontractors often reduce their prices further as compared to what they offered during bid submission. This is because now that they have known the exact winner, they would like to grab the order from this winner. In such cases the profit margins increase vis-à-vis to that considered during bid submission but Project managers are usually reluctant to consider for following two reasons;
    21→
    22→1) They would like to consider a safe and comfortable cost as illustrated before so that they have no chances of cost overrun during the execution of the project  
    23→2) They would like to take credit for increase in margins during project execution, lest some of the credit goes to the Bidding department.
    24→
    25→Once I came across such instances, I understood that it is matter of great conflict between the Bidding and the Project team. Both teams were called and were asked for united consensus. The Bidding manager was told clearly that while all costs to be considered must be competitive they must also be practical and accepted later by the vendors and subcontractors after award. The Project manager was told clearly that bidding team cannot consider safe and comfortable cost in today’s competitive world in order to win projects, they must consider competitive costs to win awards and only when they win awards for the organization, all other departments can have some work to do. Without awards, all have to remain idle and suck their thumbs and may also have to remain without salaries\! Also, another important step was introduced. While a Bid Manager was associated with the Project manager and SCM after award to finalise project cost and the budget, a Project engineer was associated to the bidding team to finalise bid cost before bid submission. In this manner, a harmony and understanding was created between these two departments.
    26→
    27→**The salient points of concern are:**
    28→
    29→1. Bid costs must be very competitive to win awards. They must be, however, be practical and acceptable later by the vendors on award  
    30→2. Associating Project engineer with bidding team during bid submission and a Bidding engineer with project team upon award will create harmony between departments and can arrive at a consensus budget.
    31→

     2→title: "PROJECT MANAGER IGNORING CONTRACT DOCUMENTS"
     3→part: 8
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "2279-2302"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : PROJECT MANAGER IGNORING CONTRACT DOCUMENTS**
     9→
    10→This is a big issue I have observed happening across corporates. We know that a contractor awarded with a contract is provided with the signed contract documents by the customer. Contract documents comprise of the Letter of Award (LoA), bidding documents and Minutes of Meeting (MoM) of pre-award discussions.
    11→
    12→There is a process in organisations called the Bid to Contract handover. In this process, the bid Manager hands over all bidding calculations to derive at cost and prices, vendors considered with reasons, pre-bid survey report, all necessary assumptions of project execution and also the entire contract documents comprising of bidding documents, LoA and MoM. The Project team now takes over the contact from the bidding team.
    13→
    14→However, in a great enthusiasm to head start, the Project Manager rushes to plan the execution of the project and mobilise site to commence the works. He does not take care to go through the contract documents thoroughly or the details provided by the bidding team during handover.
    15→
    16→Say for example, the documents stipulate that payment for those supplies shall not be given if those supplies made have not been installed within one month of those materials received at site. This is to stop malpractice of contractors who try to supply materials well in advance and are kept in stores for long duration without installation. 
    17→
    18→Say, the Project Manager is ignorant of this clause and to satisfy monthly targets, he advances some supplies to generate revenues. Thereafter on billing or being informed by Accounts, he understands that such supplies will not be paid for at that point of time since installation of those supplied materials will commence much later on. Therefore the payment gets stuck. In the meantime, the supplier can ask for its payments as per his payment terms with the contractor. This situation will lead to severe negative cash flow which an organisation never wishes to happen.
    19→
    20→Therefore, a Project Manager must go through the contract documents minutely and thereafter plan his works. Such enthusiasm without proper understanding of contractual terms and conditions will not be appreciated by an organisation.   
    21→
    22→Orders placed by the client also comprises of the bidding specifications based on which the project was bid along with other contractual terms and conditions. Such orders have voluminous documents which must be read by the Project Manager and other relevant Heads minutely. There are several penalties stipulated in an order for violation of contractual terms and conditions and therefore studying contractual documents is of significant importance. 
    23→
    24→I have often seen that even “so called good” Project Managers dive into their projects right from day one to execute the project on time. While this intention is appreciated, it is also pathetic to observe that these Project Managers do not read the contract meticulously and often miss important contractual terms which may prove to be fatal to the project and the organization.
    25→
    26→**The salient points of concern are:**
    27→
    28→1. Bid to Contract handover by the bidding team to the Project team is a important matter. It must be taken up seriously with interactions to understand the project and its assumptions made during the bid  
    29→2. Contract documents must be read thoroughly by the Project Manager and relevant Heads so as to drive the project in a proper contractual manner  
    30→3. Severe penalties as stipulated in contract documents may be levied for violation of contractual terms and conditions. The Project Manager and the Contracts team must be aware.
    31→

1→---
     2→title: "INTENTIONAL PRE-TIMED EARLY SUPPLIES"
     3→part: 8
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "2303-2331"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : INTENTIONAL PRE-TIMED EARLY SUPPLIES**
     9→
    10→This is a common malpractice of Project Management people. There are usually many kinds of materials and products to be supplied in a project. These materials and products are required at different time periods of the project execution. Some of these are required early, some are required late and some may be required almost throughout the duration of the project.
    11→
    12→We know the theory of JIT, ie. Just in Time. Now, the Project Managers are given monthly revenue targets by the SBU Head. Since the project execution schedules are very stiff, some project execution may be delayed, particularly on the installation front. Therefore there is a shortfall in the monthly revenue targets. So often many Project Managers cunningly (preplanned) supplies certain products to site stores which would actually be required much later for the installation of the same. By such a process, he generates revenues for such pre-timed supplies and tries to recover the otherwise monthly revenue shortfall.
    13→
    14→The pitfalls of such practices are as follows:
    15→
    16→1\. Today many clients have incorporated in contracts a mechanism called “construction linked progressive supply payment”. Against the earlier normal typical payment term of Supply like 10% advance against Bank Guarantees, 80% progressive payment against supplies and balance 10% retention money after completion of project against bank guarantee, this mechanism may split the progressive payment of Supply in two parts. It could be 60% progressive payment against supplies made (even if pre-timed) but the balance 40% payment of supplied materials would be made after the contractor installs those supplied materials. Therefore if you do not construct or install soon enough the entire lot of materials supplied, then your 40% payment for supplies gets stuck\!
    17→
    18→2\. Advancing material supplies unnecessarily chokes the stores and increase inventory costs.
    19→
    20→3\. Handling and dispatching materials from stores to site locations become tedious because the stores are filled up with materials required at a later stage of project execution.
    21→
    22→4. Often vendors do not agree to the clause of balance 40% progressive payment on installation of the supplied material since they argue that installation is not in their scope of work. So the contractor is stuck with the 40% payment for supplies which it will receive upon installation, yet the vendor claims 100% payment after their supplies. This leads to negative cash flow.  
    23→5. Materials lying in stores but required much later on may be damaged or rusted over a period of time. This may require replacing materials once again escalating unnecessary costs. 
    24→
    25→Therefore, while advancing supplies helps Project Managers and leaders above him to somehow meet monthly revenue targets, the organisation is seriously affected in handling negative cash flow and the other perils listed above or to take loans from banks to mitigate the problem. In any case, the organization incurs unnecessary additional costs. 
    26→
    27→Project Managers need to stop this malpractice. Maybe once a while it may be okay for a month at the fag end of the year to meet the annual targets, but doing so regularly for many months will have serious repercussions. It’s like smoking once daily for a week may not have any health risk but doing so regularly for many months may lead to lung cancer. Leaders at the top must encourage SBU Heads and Project Managers not to resort to such malpractices even if there is a monthly revenue shortfall. After all, interest of the organization far supersedes meeting monthly revenues through undesired means affecting the organization. 
    28→
    29→**The salient points of concern are:**
    30→
    31→1. As far as practically possible, JIT supplies should be followed as a practice.  
    32→2. Malpractice to pre-time supplies must be avoided, particularly when there is a Bid to Contract handover by the bidding team to the Project team is a construction linked progressive supply payment stipulated in the contract document.  
    33→3. Pre-timed supplies unnecessarily choke stores making its operations difficult.  
    34→4. Pre-timed supplies may be damaged or rusted over a period of time  
    35→5. Interest of the organization is of utmost importance; it far supersedes meeting monthly revenues through undesired means affecting the organization.
    36→

     2→title: "IGNORANCE TO PROJECT HEALTH"
     3→part: 8
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "2332-2347"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : IGNORANCE TO PROJECT HEALTH**
     9→
    10→While the Project Manager in the office and the Site Manager in the site thrives to execute the project, often the Project Manager ignore monitoring essential data of progress. Therefore I have evolved what I call as the Project Health Card. Just like our body health card comprises of blood test results and other reports like ECG, Ultrasound, Chest X-Ray, etc, similarly, a Project Health Card would determine various parameters of the Project in progress, ie. the current health of the project. 
    11→
    12→Project Health Card has parameters easy to understand and effectively drive the project in directions where it lags. It is practically easy to implement these lag parameters rather than following measures we often learn in management theories which are difficult to implement.
    13→
    14→Project and Site Managers in their continuous drive to progress the project to completion often miss out on some key health parameters from time to time and end up in project losses at the end of the project. So that this does not happen, the Project Health Card comes to their rescue.
    15→
    16→The Project Health Card is discussed in details in a separate section of the book.
    17→
    18→   **The salient points of concern are:**
    19→
    20→1. A Project Health Card (PHC) is a list of parameters of the health of the project in progress just like our body health card issued by hospitals.  
    21→2. PHC is a monitoring mechanism for a project in progress  
    22→3. PHC is discussed in a separate section of this book
    23→

     2→title: "Some Good Practices"
     3→part: 8
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "2348-2361"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**SOME GOOD PRACTICES**
     9→
    10→**Meticulous planning**  
    11→It is said that a good plan is half the job done. Do not jump into execution as soon as a project is awarded. Plan the execution before commencing execution, better if you plan it before anticipated award.
    12→
    13→**Involve all stakeholders during planning**  
    14→Internal stake holders comprise of your HR team, Designs and Engineering team, Supply Chain, Contracts, Finance and many other departments. Effective communication amongst all stakeholders is the key to a good project management.  
    15→
    16→**Review profitability every month project wise**  
    17→A business organization executes for profits, not for honorary purpose. Hence it is the duty of the Project Manager to review profitability of projects every month. In case there are reasons of losses or decreased profits, the Manager should inform his senior about the same and discuss on mitigation measures if possible.  
    18→
    19→**Review requirement of crashing to avoid LD**  
    20→In case progress of the project is slow due to reasons attributable to the organization and time of completion is nearby, then there is a likelihood of LD being imposed on the contractor. To avoid such a situation, crashing of the project may be considered well in time. Crashing may be costlier than the original estimates, but its expenses could be still lower than the amount of LD that could be imposed on the contractor. A judicial decision in this respect will be required.
    21→
