     2→title: "Introduction - Site Operations"
     3→part: 9
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "2364-2381"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**SITE OPERATIONS**
     9→
    10→**Role of Site Operations in a corporate**  
    11→Site Operations ensure that all physical locations like manufacturing plant and stores are coordinated efficiently including the transport arrangements and availability of manpower, machinery and equipment for a seamless operations at site. Site team manager is responsible for daily site operations, managing site budgets, coordinating with different departments and importantly maintaining ethical, quality and safety standards at site.  
    12→
    13→In the earlier chapters, you have already noted the difference between Project Management & Site management. Site management relates to management of project site only and will deal with people posted at the site, the equipment and machinery available at site, the material resources brought to site from the stores and coordinate between manufacturing plant and the stores from where it receives materials.
    14→
    15→Therefore Site management is the management of the following M’s
    16→
    17→1) Man (People)  
    18→2) Machinery (Equipment/Tools)  
    19→3) Materials (Resources)  
    20→4) Money (Finance)
    21→
    22→The objective of Site management is Project Execution. It is the only department in an organisation which actually realises the turnover and profit for the company. However good an organisation is in its Leadership, Strategic and HR Management, it is all useless if the execution team falters. It is only through the execution of projects that you actually earn revenues for the company. This revenue has two components, one is the total costs (direct costs and indirect costs and overheads) of a project which it has to meet and the balance portion is the profit which the organization shall use for employee benefits including salary and invest for the future growth of the organization. Therefore excellence in Project execution is a must, otherwise the objective of all Leadership and Strategic Management fails. 
    23→
    24→Leadership must ensure that there are capable people to execute projects in a profitable manner as envisaged. HR must be serious in posting capable people at site. He should remember that his own salary too depends on this site manpower\!\!\!
    25→

1→---
     2→title: "UNETHICAL SITE MANAGERS"
     3→part: 9
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "2382-2413"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : UNETHICAL SITE MANAGERS**
     9→
    10→Some Site Managers resort to unethical practices at site. Project Managers must be aware of this. Such Site Managers do not only commit crime for pursuing unethical practices and dishonest deals but also bring disrepute to the organisation.
    11→
    12→Often there is dilemma because some of these unethical Site Managers are proven good performers. So the organisation is in a fix to take action against these individuals. This is where the principle of an organisation is put to test. The organisation may suffer initially if it has to remove such individuals from its organisation, but to maintain dignity and reputation of the organisation such actions must be taken. There is no dearth of capable individuals across our country or the globe. The HR should be quick enough to find replacements but in such cases an organisation must be ready to suffer initially to live up to its reputation.
    13→
    14→Some of the unethical practices of Site Managers are :
    15→
    16→**Faking Quotes**  
    17→Sometimes Site Managers need to gather competitive quotes for particular kind of activity required for site execution, say for example, for casting of foundations. Now, there could be a subcontractor for the work having different registered names of companies submitting different quotes under those names, but essentially the quotes being from the same entity. Dishonest Site Managers accept such quotes for vested interest and forward them to their Project Managers. In case such a malpractice is not identified, then even the lowest quote may be higher than the actual market price. Thereby the organisation lose out on profits.
    18→
    19→**Colluding with client**  
    20→Sometimes a Site Manager of an organisation may collude with the Site Manager of the client and share unethically earned money. Say for example, the Site managers of the organization and the client agree to use lesser cement in casting of foundations and sell the saved quantity of cement in the market or elsewhere and earn money. In such a case, neither the Project Manager nor the Client sitting in their offices may be aware of such malpractice. Therefore regular sudden visits to sites are recommended not only to ensure smooth functioning of the site but also to watch for any malpractice being undertaken. The Audit department may also be vigilant on such matters.
    21→
    22→**Selling materials to site**  
    23→Sometimes a Site Manager alone or colluding with client representative at  
    24→Site may use lesser quality of materials, say for example, lesser quantity of cement for foundation casting, and sell secretly the saved amount of materials in the market. The Project Manager has to use his intelligence to find out any kind of such malpractice. 
    25→
    26→**Hiding Accidents**  
    27→Human life is important. Investments and arrangements for safety must be done at site to have a Zero Tolerance Policy for accidents or deaths. However, unscrupulous site managers sometimes tend to hide accidents through unfair means to avoid punishment or removal of such manager from the organisation. Hiding accidents is not only a criminal offence by the individual but it also brings disrepute to the organisation. Project Managers in office supervising Site Managers at site must be careful to ensure that all safety measures are taken at site. Organisation and not an individual Site Manager is of larger importance. Human lives, be it of a labour or a Director, is utmost important.  
    28→
    29→**Using Child Labour**  
    30→Using child labour and hiding such labour in the books is a serious offence against the laws of the country. Mundane jobs, often which could be executed by child labour, must be provided to eligible labour. Child labour is used to curtail costs of product and services but such acts in defiance to the laws of the country must be avoided. An organisation must be able to find different strategies to optimise costs and certainly not optimise by using child labour. If it cannot find ways to be competitive, it will anyways lose to their competitors in a matter of time. So there is no use to adopt unlawful activities to optimise cost, rather it is better to have a positive outlook to find other strategic means for cost optimisation in order to challenge its competitors.
    31→
    32→**Untimely Payment**  
    33→Payment on time is a right of the subcontractors working for you. Sometimes such subcontractors requiring money on time to sustain their livelihood are exploited by delaying payments. It is also the duty of the customers to ensure timely payment to contractors so that the contractors too can make their payments to its appointed subcontractors on time. Often delayed payment to subcontractors by contractor is attributed to delayed payment to contractors by the client. However a good ethical contractor should always pay to their subcontractors on time because many livelihoods depend on it and the contractor may build in finance costs in their prices for anticipated payment delays from the client. 
    34→
    35→**The salient points of concern are:**
    36→
    37→1. Strict action including removal of employees must be practiced by top leadership and HR for unethical means employed by employees even if the employees happen to be good performers for the organization.  
    38→2. Project Manager, HR Head and Audit team must be vigilant for tracing any unethical means adopted by employees, whether in offices or at sites.
    39→

1→---
     2→title: "CRASHING IN THE LAST MOMENT"
     3→part: 9
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "2414-2430"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : CRASHING IN THE LAST MOMENT**
     9→
    10→Crashing of a project is required at times. Today the timelines for completion of projects are so tight that even the best efforts often do not help the project to be completed on time. Thus crashing project becomes a necessity. 
    11→
    12→However, the bidding team, while submitting their bid, must discuss project timelines and consider such costs of crashing in their bids. Not considering costs of crashing in bids will of course reduce the bid price but will steer the organisation towards loss or reduction in anticipated profits when such crashing needs to be employed at site..
    13→
    14→But such crashing is sometimes project-made and avoidable. When a project commences, often the enthusiasm to head start is missing or the project manpower and infrastructure is not in place and therefore the project goes slow right from the beginning. Designs and testing often take a longer time than anticipated and the project executing team gets delayed in commencing their works.
    15→
    16→Progress of project must be monitored with time. A Project Manager must understand the need of crashing a project at the correct time. Deciding crashing at a late stage of the project will lead to mess and incur too high costs. 
    17→
    18→Project execution must start like a 100 meter race. If you see the sprinters in action, the first microsecond push often helps them to win the race by microseconds. That’s the way a project commencement needs to start. Yes, there may be lot of work before a project can commence, like the manpower, finance, equipment and designs needs to be in place, but all these activities must be completed on time so that the project execution can commence and complete on time. An often lackluster attitude at the beginning of the project must be avoided.  
    19→
    20→**The salient points of concern are:**
    21→
    22→1. Bidding team must discuss with Project team the need to crash a project midway. Ignoring such costs, if required, just to make the bid more competitive and win is meaningless because in any case after you win the project, you have to incur loss or diminished profits.  
    23→2. Crashing must be considered at the right time by the Project Manager. Delayed crashing may result in a mess incurring too high costs.
    24→

     2→title: "Some Good Practices"
     3→part: 9
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "2431-2444"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**SOME GOOD PRACTICES**
     9→
    10→**Consider demographic trends**  
    11→Consider local labour as far as possible. This will help in boosting the local economy as well as ensure a happy work environment at site.
    12→
    13→**Meticulous planning**  
    14→Site Manager may discuss with Project Manager and chalk out detailed L2, L3, L4, L5 (and so on) networks so that you can monitor the minutest works from time to time to complete various works.
    15→
    16→**Resource planning**  
    17→Resources like manpower, equipment and machinery, materials, water, finance must be worked out in advance to receive them at site on time. The Project Manager have to knock different departments of the organization to ensure that these resources arrive at site on time.   
    18→   
    19→**Entertainment of workers**  
    20→Maintaining a good working relationship with workers/labour force. Good understanding between different stakeholders will boost the performance and progress of projects. Organise picnics, get-togethers and institute a system of rewards to recognize good performance
    21→

14→So all the time they concentrate primarily on the construction and the requirements for such construction from several departments. That is why they also spend lot of time at sites to ensure progress. What they miss out on most importantly is the profitability and progress of the project. Progress of a project is very important, but NOT AT THE COST of profitability and time. They must sit with Accounts and Finance from time to time to keep a track of the profitability and the construction time spent for the project. Let me discuss these two very important aspects one by one.
    15→
    16→Project Managers must understand that the final objective of an organisation is not to execute projects, but to earn money through execution of projects. Therefore Project execution is only a medium to earn money. You cannot lose sight of the final objective of an organisation. Yes, you must track the construction progress, no one disputes that, but simultaneously you have to track at what costs you are progressing the projects. Profitability has to be tracked from time to time. Project Managers have to discuss with their seniors and Finance and track the profitability vis-a-vis what was envisaged during approved bid submission. A lot of time has to be spent in thinking how to sustain and improve profitability. The actual execution of the project is the SOLE duty of the site manager whereas reviewing the execution at site is ONE OF THE duties of a Project Manager in office.
